instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
What should be done if the nominee of your term insurance is no more?
In a term insurance plan, primary coverage is offered against premature death. This means that if the insured dies during the policy tenure, a guaranteed benefit is paid to the family. But who receives the benefit in the absence of the life insured? The policyholder or the nominee?
There might be confusion in understanding who is entitled to receive the death benefit from a term insurance policy. While many of you must know that the nominee receives the benefit, there is an option for the policyholder too. Moreover, legal heirs can also make a claim for the death benefit. So, let’s understand.
Who is entitled to receive the death benefit of a term insurance policy?
Generally, the individual who can receive the term insurance benefit is the nominee. However, there might be other claimants too. Let’s understand –
If you buy the policy on your own life
If you buy the term plan for your own life, it is recommended to nominate an individual to receive the death benefit. If you don’t do so, your legal heirs can claim the death benefit. However, your legal heirs would have to submit a succession certificate1 for filing a claim.
If you buy the policy on another’s life
If you buy the term plan on the life of your spouse or children and you pay the premium, you become the policyholder while your loved one becomes the life insured. In this case, the nomination is not needed because of the insured dies, you, the policyholder, would receive the claim.
Nomination in life insurance:
A life insured nominates an individual or entity to be the nominee and receive the death benefit. Remember, the nominee might not be the beneficiary of the policy. This means that the nominee might have the right to collect the death benefit but not use it.
You can appoint multiple term insurance nominees. In that case, you have to specify the share of each nominee.
You can nominate a minor, too, like your child. However, in this case, you would have to appoint an appointee who would act on the minor’s behalf till the nominee reaches maturity.
The nominee can be changed during the policy tenure.
Implications of the nominee’s death during the policy term
The nominee might die during the term of the policy. In that case, you have the option to change the nominee. You can inform the insurance company about changing the nominee and then make the change in the policy. The change of the nominee would be recorded through an endorsement of the original policy.
Appointing a minor as the nominee
It is possible to appoint a minor, i.e., an individual aged below 18 years, as a nominee. In that case, however, you would have to appoint another individual to act as an appointee2. The appointee should be 18 years or more. The appointee would act on behalf of the minor nominee till the nominee becomes an adult. In case the insured dies when the nominee is still a minor, the appointee would have to collect the death benefit on the minor’s behalf.
The implication of not having a nominee under the plan
Having a nominee is beneficial since it helps in faster claim settlements. However, the nomination is not mandatory. If you don’t have a nominee, your legal heirs can claim the death benefit in your absence. However, your legal heirs would have to submit the succession certificate1. This certificate is issued by the court, and it establishes the claimant’s rights over the death benefit.
However, getting a succession certificate might take time. The legal heirs would have to approach a court, submit the relevant documents and then get the certificate issued.
As such, having a term insurance nominee is an easier alternative.
Conclusion
The nominee eases up the claim settlement process. If you nominate your immediate family, the nominee can also become the beneficiary and use the death benefit to take care of the family’s financial needs and obligations. So, appoint a nominee when buying the insurance policy. You can also nominate during the term of the plan and change the nomination if needed.
If the nominee dies, appoint a new nominee but ensure that there is a valid nomination so that the claim process becomes smoother for your family when you are not around.
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. output.final_text:
What should be done if the nominee of your term insurance is no more?
In a term insurance plan, if the insured dies during the policy tenure, a guaranteed* benefit is paid to the family. But who receives the benefit in the absence of the life insured? The policyholder or the nominee?
There might be confusion in understanding who is entitled to receive the death benefit from a term insurance policy. While many of you must know that the nominee receives the benefit, there is an option for the policyholder too. Moreover, legal heirs can also make a claim for the death benefit. So, let’s understand.
Who receives the death benefit of a term insurance policy?
Generally, the nominee receives the death benefit of a term plan. However, some cases might be different. Let’s understand –
1. If you buy the policy for yourself
If you buy the term plan on your own life, the nominee stated in the policy receives the death benefit on happening of the insured event during the policy term. However, if you did not appoint a nominee, your legal heirs can claim the death benefit. In such cases, your legal heirs would have to submit a succession certificate1 for filing a claim. 2. If you buy the policy for someone else
If you buy the term plan on the life of your spouse or children and you pay the premium, you become the policyholder.. In this case, if the life insured dies, you receive the death benefit. Nomination in life insurance:
A life assured nominates an individual or entity to be the nominee who will receive the death benefit in case of unfortunate death of the life assured during policy term.
You may appoint multiple nominees under a term insurance plan. In that case, you have to specify the share of each nominee.
1. You can nominate a minor too, like your child. However, in this case, you would have to
appoint an appointee who would act on the minor’s behalf till the nominee reaches maturity. 2. The nominee can be changed during the policy tenure.
Implications of the nominee’s death during the policy term
In some situations, the nominee might die during the term of the policy. In that case, you have the option to change the nominee. You can inform the insurance company about changing the nominee. The change of the nominee would be recorded through an endorsement of the original policy.
Appointing a minor as the nominee
It is possible to appoint a minor, i.e., an individual aged below 18 years, as a nominee. In that case, however, you would have to appoint another individual to act as an appointee2. The appointee should be 18 years or more. The appointee would act on behalf of the minor nominee till the
nominee becomes an adult. In case the insured dies when the nominee is still a minor, the appointee would have to collect the death benefit on the minor’s behalf.
The implication of not having a nominee under the plan
Having a nominee is beneficial since it helps in convenient claim settlements. If you don’t have a nominee, your legal heirs may claim the death benefit in your absence. However, your legal heirs would have to submit the succession certificate1. This certificate is issued by the court, and it establishes the claimant’s rights over the death benefit.
The legal heirs would have to approach a competent court, submit the relevant documents and then get the certificate issued.
As such, having a term insurance nominee is an preferred alternative.
Conclusion
The nominee eases up the claim settlement process. If you nominate your immediate family, the nominee may also become the beneficiary and use the death benefit to take care of the family’s financial needs and obligations. So, appoint a nominee when buying the life insurance policy. You may also nominate during the term of the plan and change the nomination if needed.
If the nominee dies during the policy term, the policyholder may appoint a new nominee so that the claim process becomes smoother for your family when you are not around.
